chunks.page_summary: This document provides guidance and best practices for preparing wiring for Rheonics Ex intrinsically safe sensors, covering cable crimping and terminal connections for various viscometers and density meters, particularly when custom-length cables are supplied without pre-assembled connectors.
chunks.extracted_text:
Phone: +1 713 234 0863

Wire preparation for Rheonics Ex Sensor

Modified on: Thu, 17 Oct, 2024 at 7:15 PM

What products are involved?
SRV (https://rheonics.com/products/inline-viscometer-srv/), SRD (https://rheonics.com/products/inline-density-meter-srd/), DVM (https://rheonics.com/products/hpht-pvt-density-meter-viscometer-dvm/), DVP (https://rheonics.com/products/inline-gas-lng-density-meter-dvp/), SME-DRM (https://rheonics.com/electronics-and-communication/), SME-TR/TRD. (https://rheonics.com/electronics-and-communication/)
For SME-DRM (DIN rail mount) transmitter. Visit our product page to learn more about the full features.
Visit Rheonics product page: Products » inline fluid viscometer and density meter (https://rheonics.com/products/)

What is the purpose of this article?
To give guidance and good practices when preparing Rheonics EX intrinsically safe sensor cable with a crimp of length 8mm and terminal diameter 1mm. The terminals of the SME are rated for wires of 0.2 - 1.5 mm2 i.e. AWG 24 - 16.
Normally, Rheonics provides the Ex cables completely assembled and ready for installation, so the user can omit the next sections and only focus on the proper wiring. For cases where the Ex cables are not delivered assembled with the M12 connector and pin ferrules (i.e. when Rheonics delivers a roll of cable for many custom lengths), follow the next sections.

Intrinsically Safe Viscometer
rheonics

Figure 1. Intrinsically Safe Viscometer

1. Required tools
Tools
key_facts: The document provides guidance and good practices for preparing Rheonics EX intrinsically safe sensor cables.
Rheonics normally provides Ex cables completely assembled and ready for installation, which may allow users to omit subsequent sections if not performing custom cable assembly.
This guide is specifically for instances where Ex cables are not delivered pre-assembled with M12 connectors and pin ferrules, such as when supplied as a roll of cable for custom lengths.
The terminals of the SME are rated for wires with a cross-section of 0.2 - 1.5 mm² (AWG 24 - 16).
The SRV Viscometer can be installed in Zone 0, Zone 1, Zone 2, or a Safe Zone.
Zener Barriers can be placed in Zone 2 or a Safe Zone.
Electronics must be located only in a Safe Zone.
diagram_labels: Zener Barriers (can be in Zone 2 or Safe)
SRV Viscometer (can be in Zone 0,1,2 or Safe)
warnings_notes: The document pertains to "Rheonics EX intrinsically safe sensor cable" and features "Ex" and "IECEx" logos, indicating compliance with intrinsic safety standards for use in hazardous areas.

Critical safety placement guidelines are provided for components: Zener Barriers are restricted to Zone 2 or Safe Zone; Electronics must exclusively be in a Safe Zone; the SRV Viscometer is certified for placement in Zone 0, 1, 2, or Safe Zone.

chunks.page_summary: This page describes various hand tools, including a crimping tool, a precision micro wire cutter, a measuring tape, and a stripping tool, outlining their functions and common applications.
chunks.extracted_text:
• Crimping Tool
Crimping tools are used to deform materials and create connections. To connect wires together or to other connectors, crimping is commonly used in electrical work.

Figure 2. Crimping Tool - 24-10 AWG [1]

• Precision Micro Wire Cutter
A wire cutter or cable cutter is a tool that cut wires and cables in a safe manner without damaging the insulation or conductors.

Figure 3. Precision Micro Wire Cutter. [2]

• Measuring Tape
A Measure Tape is a tool used to measure length.

Figure 4. Measuring Tape [3]

• Stripping tool
Wire strippers are portable handheld tools used by workers, especially electricians, to remove the protective coating of electric wires for replacement or repair.
key_facts: Crimping tools are used to deform materials and create connections, commonly in electrical work to connect wires.
A wire cutter or cable cutter is a tool for safely cutting wires and cables without damaging insulation or conductors.
A Measure Tape is a tool used to measure length.
Wire strippers are portable handheld tools used by electricians to remove the protective coating of electric wires for replacement or repair.

Heat guns (hot air guns) are devices that emit a jet of compressed air toward a particular point or surface.

chunks.page_summary: This page provides details on common electrical installation components including metal tape, ferrules, solder wire, and heat shrink tubing, along with their specifications and typical usage within Rheonics' practices.
chunks.extracted_text:

Reference
Metal tape
3M
4312555
302-03-552
https://www.distrelec.ch/en/aluminium-foil-tape-431-25mm-55m-silver-3m-4312555/p/30203552
(https://www.distrelec.ch/en/aluminium-foil-tape-431-25mm-55m-silver-3m-4312555/p/30203552)
Ferrule
Correct ferrule size must be selected based on the gauge of the wire, as a standard Rheonics uses ferrule with a pin length of 8mm and diameter of 1mm (cross-section 0.5mm2).
Figure 9. 22 AWG (8mm Pin) Insulated Ferrules [8]
Solder wire
Solder wires are wires with a low melting point, used to create joints using a soldering iron.
Figure 10. Solder wire [9]
Heat shrink tube
Heat shrink tubing is a common element in most electrical installations that protects wires from moisture, dust, abrasion, and sharp objects that might otherwise damage them.
key_facts: Correct ferrule size must be selected based on the gauge of the wire.
Rheonics standard practice is to use ferrules with a pin length of 8mm and a diameter of 1mm (cross-section 0.5mm2).
Solder wires are characterized by a low melting point and are used for creating electrical joints with a soldering iron.
Heat shrink tubing is used in electrical installations to protect wires from environmental factors like moisture, dust, abrasion, and physical damage from sharp objects.

chunks.page_summary: This page details the specifications and types of cables, connectors, and sealing methods used with Rheonics SRV/SRD sensors, including components for potentially hazardous (Ex) environments.
chunks.extracted_text:
Figure 11. Hear shrink tube [10]

Ex Sensor Cable

The SRV/SRD sensor is connected to its associated Zener diode barriers by means of a cable that has an 8-pole M12 connector on the sensor end.

The cable and connector selected must be rated for at least the highest ambient temperature at which the sensor will be used.

HELUKABEL® OB-BL-PAAR-CY 4x2x0.5 QMM / 14079 900 V CE

Figure 12. Ex sensor cable[11]

M12 connector of the sensor cable

Connector, Universal, 8-position, halogen-free, shielded, Socket straight M12, A-coded, Insulation displacement connection, knurl material: Zinc die-cast, nickel-plated, external cable diameter 5 mm ... 9.7 mm

Figure 13. M12 connector [12]

Metal tape

This tape is commonly used to secure duct seams, connections, and joints. Additionally, it has excellent waterproof properties.
key_facts: The SRV/SRD sensor connects to Zener diode barriers using an 8-pole M12 connector on the sensor end.
The cable and connector must be rated for the highest ambient temperature at which the sensor will be used.
The M12 connector is universal, 8-position, halogen-free, shielded, straight, A-coded, with an insulation displacement connection.
The M12 connector's knurl material is zinc die-cast and nickel-plated.
Metal tape is commonly used to secure duct seams, connections, and joints, and has excellent waterproof properties.
warnings_notes: The cable and connector selected must be rated for at least the highest ambient temperature at which the sensor will be used.

chunks.page_summary: This page provides step-by-step instructions for preparing a sensor cable, including stripping the sheath, fixing the shield with metal tape, and connecting individual wires to a color-coded connector.
chunks.extracted_text:
Figure 16. The sensor cable after the cable sheath is stripped away on the first 80mm

*   Since the sensor cable has an outer diameter of about 8.5 mm, the cable's shield must be fixed with the provided metal tape as shown in the instructions for cable with a diameter over 6 mm.

Figure 17. Sensor cable fixed with metal tape

*   After the cable shield has been fixed, the rest of the shield can be cut at the edge of the tape. Then pass all 8 wires of the cable through the connection body. Take care not to damage the insulation of the wires. Push the cable completely into the sensor connector to ensure proper contact with the cable shield.
*   The connector body has 8 color marked slits in the front. Pull each wire into the slit marked with its color as shown in Figure 18.

Figure 18: The connector body is shown from the front. It has 8 slits which are color marked according to wire color.

*   Cut the remnant of each wire, so each wire is fixed in the available space.
key_facts: The sensor cable sheath should be stripped by 80mm.
The sensor cable shield, having an outer diameter of 8.5 mm, must be secured with metal tape, as required for cables exceeding 6 mm in diameter.
After securing the cable shield, the remaining shield material should be trimmed at the tape's edge.
All 8 wires of the cable must be passed through the connection body.
The cable should be pushed completely into the sensor connector to ensure proper contact between the cable shield and the connector.
The connector body has 8 color-marked slits in the front, and each wire should be pulled into the slit corresponding to its color.
After insertion, the remnant of each wire should be cut to fix it securely in place.
The connector body has 8 slits which are color marked according to wire color.
diagram_labels: Figure 16. The sensor cable after the cable sheath is stripped away on the first 80mm
Figure 17. Sensor cable fixed with metal tape
Figure 18: The connector body is shown from the front. It has 8 slits which are color marked according to wire color.
warnings_notes: Take care not to damage the insulation of the wires.
Push the cable completely into the sensor connector to ensure proper contact with the cable shield.

chunks.page_summary: This page provides step-by-step instructions and required tools for preparing a sensor cable, including stripping the sheath, twisting the shield, removing internal covers, and preparing the grounding cable for connection to Zener barriers.
chunks.extracted_text:
1. Knife
2. Cutter
• Strip the blue outer cable sheath on a length of 100 mm (4 in.).
Figure 21: Preparation of the sensor cable to connect it to the Zener barriers.
• After the stripping of the cable sheath, carefully loosen and then twist the cable shield.
Figure 22. Twisted cable shield.
• Remove the plastic cover from the colored wires.
Figure 23. Removing the plastic cover from the wires.
3.1. Preparing grounding cable
Requirements:
1. Soldering machine
2. Solder wire
3. Heat Shrink tube
4. Hot air gun
• Take the twisted cable shield and cover it fully with a heat shrink.
key_facts: The blue outer cable sheath must be stripped for a length of 100 mm (4 in.).
After stripping the cable sheath, the cable shield needs to be carefully loosened and twisted.
The plastic cover must be removed from the colored wires inside the cable.
Preparation of the grounding cable requires a soldering machine, solder wire, heat shrink tube, and a hot air gun.
The twisted cable shield should be fully covered with a heat shrink.
diagram_labels: Figure 21: Preparation of the sensor cable to connect it to the Zener barriers.

chunks.page_summary: This page provides step-by-step instructions for preparing cable shields and ground cables with heat shrink and ferrules, and details the process for installing crimpers on sensor wires, including tool usage and handling of unused wires.
chunks.extracted_text:
- Take the twisted cable shield and cover it fully with a heat shrink.
Figure 24. Metal shield twisted with heat shrink.
- Crimp a ferrule to the ground cable end.
Figure 25. Ferrule crimped to the ground cable

3.2. Installing crimpers in each sensor wire

Requirement:
1. Crimping tool
Your crimping tool should have a nest for inserting the terminal. After inserting in the correct slot, squeeze the crimp tool firmly and release it to get a long-lasting, tight fuse.
Unused wires should be neither stripped nor crimped. We recommend protecting the ends with a shrink tube to avoid the ends making any electrical contacts.
We recommend crimping the ferrules on 4 sides for the connection with the Zener barriers.
- Twist the wire and Crimp them (Crimpers) using the Crimping Tool.
key_facts: The twisted cable shield should be fully covered with a heat shrink.
A ferrule must be crimped to the ground cable end.
A crimping tool should have a nest for inserting the terminal.
The crimp tool should be squeezed firmly to achieve a long-lasting, tight fuse.
Ferrules should be crimped on 4 sides for the connection with Zener barriers.
Wires should be twisted and then crimped using the Crimping Tool.
diagram_labels: Figure 24. Metal shield twisted with heat shrink.
Figure 25. Ferrule crimped to the ground cable
warnings_notes: Unused wires should be neither stripped nor crimped. We recommend protecting the ends with a shrink tube to avoid the ends making any electrical contacts.

chunks.page_summary: This page provides step-by-step instructions for preparing sensor cable wire ends, including stripping, crimping ferrules, and connecting them to Zener barriers and an SME-DRM sensor, specifically for an installation in a hazardous area.
chunks.extracted_text:
Figure 26. Crimping the wire end.

• The green and yellow wires are not used to operate the sensor. They have to be connected to the ground together with the shield of the cable. This means that they should be connected to the ground terminals of the Zener barriers.

Figure 27. Strip all eight wires on a length of 10 mm and put bootlace ferrules on the ends.

• Strip all eight wires on a length of 10 mm and put bootlace ferrules on the ends.

Figure 28. Wire ends stripped with crimped ferrules.

4. Ex zener barriers to SME connection

Next is the connection of the Zener barriers with the SME-DRM, the blue end is used to connect to the sensor that is installed in the hazardous area.
key_facts: The green and yellow wires are not used for sensor operation but must be connected to ground with the cable shield.
The green and yellow wires and cable shield should be connected to the ground terminals of the Zener barriers.
All eight wires must be stripped to a length of 10 mm.
Bootlace ferrules must be applied to the ends of all eight stripped wires.
The connection involves Zener barriers and the SME-DRM.
The blue end of the SME-DRM cable connects to the sensor installed in a hazardous area.
diagram_labels: Figure 27. Strip all eight wires on a length of 10 mm and put bootlace ferrules on the ends.
Figure 28. Wire ends stripped with crimped ferrules.
warnings_notes: The sensor is installed in a hazardous area, implying specific safety and intrinsic safety considerations related to 'Ex zener barriers'.

chunks.page_summary: This page provides instructions for connecting and grounding Zener barriers and an SME-DRM unit on a DIN-rail.
chunks.extracted_text:
Figure 29. The connection between Zener barriers and SME-DRM

Requirement:
1. Various wires with crimped ends.
2. Zener barriers Z041x2 and Z757.
3. SME-DRM

• Install the SME-DRM and the Zener barriers in a DIN-rail and prepare short wires to be used.

Figure 30. Short wires.

• Next is to have a proper grounding, which could be either the DIN-rail or the ground available in the Zener barriers.

Figure 31. Zener barrier grounding.

• The wiring should be done the same as the wiring diagram shown in Figure 28 Part A. The end result should be as follow.
key_facts: The connection involves Zener barriers and an SME-DRM unit.
Requirements include various wires with crimped ends, Zener barriers (models Z041x2 and Z757), and an SME-DRM unit.
The SME-DRM and Zener barriers must be installed on a DIN-rail.
Short wires need to be prepared for the connections.
Proper grounding is required and can be achieved via the DIN-rail or the ground available in the Zener barriers.
The wiring should follow the diagram provided in Figure 28 Part A.

chunks.page_summary: This page illustrates the wiring and mounting options for Rheonics SME-DRM, Zener Barriers, and Ex Sensors, including alternative enclosure configurations.
chunks.extracted_text:
Figure 34. Full wiring of the SME-DRM+Zener Barriers+Ex Sensor cable.
Note:
Typically SME-DRM is used for connection with EX barriers so both can be mounted inside a cabinet with DIN rails
Figure 35. SME-DRM
However, it is also possible to put the EX barriers in another enclosure and connect to the SME-TR or SME-TRD using cable glands.
The barrier can also be used in an enclosure and a cable can be connected between barrier and the SME-TR/TRD through cable glands.
key_facts: SME-DRM is typically used with EX barriers and both can be mounted in a cabinet on DIN rails.
EX barriers can be placed in a separate enclosure and connected to SME-TR or SME-TRD using cable glands.
A cable can connect a barrier in an enclosure to SME-TR/TRD through cable glands.
warnings_notes: Note: Typically SME-DRM is used for connection with EX barriers so both can be mounted inside a cabinet with DIN rails
